# Figure description: скв_388.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/скв_388.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины)** — a composite well log from a Soviet-era drilling report. It is a long, vertically oriented document typical of Soviet geological survey practice, containing multiple parallel columns displaying lithological, geophysical, and sampling data recorded along the depth axis of a drilled borehole. At the bottom there is a section labeled **"КОНТРОЛЬНЫЕ КРИВЫЕ"** (Control Curves), which is a standard quality-control section appended to well logs of this type.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers borehole **"р-к Оксоновский"** (Oksanovsky prospect/borehole), as readable in the header at the top of the document. The header also contains what appears to be a survey/report number (partially legible: **№ 73** or similar on the left, **№ 76** or similar on the right). The document references work carried out in **northern Kazakhstan**, consistent with the broader M-42/N-42 survey context.

The log appears to cover a **substantial depth range** — given the physical length of the document and the density of the curve traces, likely on the order of several hundred metres (possibly 500–1000+ m), though the precise top and bottom depths are **[illegible]** due to scan quality. The depth scale runs vertically from top (shallow) to bottom (deep), as is standard.

---

## Key Content

The composite log contains the following identifiable column types, reading from left to right:

1. **Leftmost columns** — Lithological/stratigraphic description columns. These appear to include:
   - A **stratigraphy/age column** with formation unit symbols
   - A **lithological column** with graphic pattern symbols representing rock types (e.g., sandstone, shale, carbonate — patterns are visible but individual symbols are largely **[illegible]** due to scan resolution)
   - **Sample/core recovery** notations
   - Written lithological descriptions (text is largely **[illegible]**)

2. **Central geophysical curve columns** — This is the dominant visual element. There are **two or three continuous geophysical curve traces** running the full depth of the log, showing:
   - High-amplitude, irregular oscillations throughout most of the log — consistent with **electrical resistivity (КС — кажущееся сопротивление)** or **spontaneous potential (ПС)** curves
   - A notably **smoother, lower-amplitude trace** (possibly natural gamma or SP) running alongside the more jagged curve
   - A prominent **anomalous zone** roughly in the middle-lower portion of the log where the curves show a distinct lateral excursion and disruption — this may indicate a **lithological change, fracture zone, fluid contact, or mineralised interval**
   - The curves appear to shift character at several discrete depths, suggesting **formation boundaries or casing points**

3. **Right-side columns** — These appear to contain:
   - **Porosity, permeability, or core analysis data** in tabular or bar form
   - **Sampling intervals** or **perforation intervals**
   - Numerical values that are largely **[illegible]**

4. **КОНТРОЛЬНЫЕ КРИВЫЕ (Control Curves) section** at the bottom — A shortened repeat section of the geophysical curves over a selected depth interval, used in Soviet logging practice to verify instrument repeatability. Three curve traces are visible here, showing broadly consistent character with the main log above.

The most visually distinctive feature is a **zone of curve disturbance/anomaly** approximately two-thirds down the log, where one of the central curves shows a sharp lateral displacement and irregular character before resuming a more regular pattern. This is characteristic of either a **casing shoe, a washout zone, a lithological boundary, or a mineralised/altered interval**.

---

## Research Value

This composite well log provides a continuous record of subsurface lithology and geophysical response for the Oksanovsky borehole, allowing a geologist to identify formation boundaries, potential mineralised or anomalous intervals, and the general stratigraphic architecture encountered during drilling. The prominent curve anomaly in the mid-to-lower section of the log is of particular interest, as it may represent a lithological change, structural feature, or mineralised zone relevant to the mineral potential assessment of the M-42/N-42 survey area. Combined with the regional seismic and gravity data from Report 36572, this log would help constrain subsurface models and guide targeting of further exploration in northern Kazakhstan.
